chunk_summary: India-specific barriers to nutrition care in chronic cardiorespiratory diseases include late diagnosis, cost pressures, and environmental risks. Priority actions involve leveraging national health programs and caregiver-friendly feeding prescriptions to address implementation constraints.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of late diagnosis and cost pressures on nutrition care in chronic cardiorespiratory diseases in India.
Identify strategies to mitigate risks like NPO spirals and dehydration in CF patients.
Learn how national health programs like RBSK can support early diagnosis and nutrition care.
Develop caregiver-friendly feeding prescriptions to improve adherence and outcomes.
key_facts: Late diagnosis and long travel to tertiary care centers are major barriers to nutrition care in India.
Formula dilution and missed feeds due to household cost pressures worsen nutrition delivery.
Hot ambient temperatures increase sweat losses and dehydration risk in CF patients, requiring salt and fluid planning.
Early referral pathways under RBSK can help identify congenital conditions and link families to services.
Written feeding prescriptions detailing route, density, volume, and recovery plans improve real-world adherence.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Early referral pathways and child health screening programs (including RBSK under the National Health Mission) can help identify congenital conditions and link families to services.
document_key_topics: Barriers to nutrition care in India
Risks like NPO spirals and dehydration in CF
Role of national health programs in nutrition care
